title: Meb Faber: Warren Buffett Didn't Follow His Own Advice | #631

segments.text: Welcome to the Metfavor Show, where the focus is on helping you grow and preserve your
wealth.
Join us as we discuss the craft of investing and uncover new and profitable ideas, all
to help you grow wealthier and wiser.
Better investing starts here.
Metfavor is the co-founder and chief investment officer at Cambrian Investment Management.
Due to industry regulations, he will not discuss any of Cambrian funds on this podcast.
All opinions expressed by podcast participants are solely their own opinions and do not reflect
the opinion of Cambrian Investment Management or its affiliates.
For more information, visit Cambriainvestments.com.
This episode is brought to you by Ivy Invest.
You know I've talked a lot on the show about endowment style investing, the Yale model,
the Swinson Playbook, public markets, private equity, private credit, real estate, all blended
together into a portfolio built for the long game.
The data on this approach is pretty compelling.
The problem has always been the same as basically impossible for regular investors to actually
do it.
Well here's somebody who lived through that world from the inside.
Andy Lee spent 18 years managing multi-billion dollar endowment funds in New York and at some
point she had a pretty simple realization.
This is exactly how she had won investor-owned money, but even she couldn't access it
as an individual.
So she built a thing that didn't exist.
Ivy Invest is one of the first endowment style funds designed for everyday investors.
You get a single fund with exposure to private equity, real estate, private credit, and public
markets.
The whole mix starting at just a thousand bucks.
I actually had Wendy on the podcast, go back and listen to that episode, it's worth your
time.
For now check them out at ivyinvest.co, that's ivyinvest.co.
Before investing carefully consider the investment objective, risk, charges, and expenses of the
institutional investment strategy fund.
The prospectus contains this and other information, past performance does not guarantee future
results.
Learn more at ivyinvest.co.
Hey everyone, meb here, quick note from today's episode is a little bit different.
I was in Omaha recently to speak at the CFA Society and while I was in town, I sat down

Hope you enjoyed as much as I enjoyed doing it.
Here we go.
Welcome back to compounding wisdom with Jared and Geo.
We have a super special guest with you today.
We have meb favor in the house.

That's trademark.
We're in the backyard of Uncle Warren's house and there's it's Berkshire Weeks, so we
have hundreds of thousands of people coming down, taking pictures of Warren's little cottage
and getting $2 daily bars and so it's going to be a fun time so thanks for stopping by
meb.
Great to be here.
And I think you have some Nebraska roots and you're a farm bore yourself, huh?
My dad was a Husker.
He was born in the tiny town of 200 people.
I mean, we joke that he grew up in the house with no running water and outhouse, you know,
a different time.
We spent a lot of time on the farm in my early days.
My first beer was in Nebraska, is disgusting, you know, and had a lot of formative memories
from being out here.
So we're going to talk about a lot of the things that you chatted with us about at the
CFA Nebraska dinner.
You offended everyone in the room and so it was a good time.
And one of them was just the Berkshire people.
Berkshire is known, well Warren Buffett is known for saying that, you know, if I don't
invest in Berkshire stock, I'm sure you're going to put all my money in the S&P 500.
And that doesn't sound like the best advice for everyone and why is that?
First of all, let me start with my favorite statistic in the entire world that sounds totally
unbelievable.
And you guys can check me on it.
I had to run this in Excel after my buddy, Chris Blumesran, has a statistic that he said,
Berkshire Hathaway, since inception, relative to the S&P, could decline 99% and still be
outperforming the S&P since inception.
Unbelivable.
And I think it's actually like 99.5%, but I'm just rounding.
The point being is that compounding that gap widens over time.
So 20% over time obviously gets bigger and bigger.
Let's forward guess what?
You're the richest person in the world.
So you may want to listen to him and not me.
I think Warren, if you look at his long history, he didn't do that.
He did all sorts of fairly sophisticated trading.
I mean, even now in his late retiree years, he was gassing up the plane and going to Japan
and buying stocks and companies over there.
So, you know, he didn't really follow his own advice and there's a lot of things that he
said that I think the rest of the world, it's kind of do what I do not what I say.
And what he was trying to do there if I'm speaking for him is he's airing on simplicity.
And it's true over 50, 100 years, you'll be totally fine just investing in something
like the S&P.
And that's been true since the inception of the Republic of 1800, right?
You know, really when the first stock started to get listed back then, if you had to
ask me, though, I don't think it's optimal or the best way to go about it.
If you're going to close your eyes, hold your nose for 20, 50, 100 years, go for it.
But most of us don't live that way.
Well, there's a ton of concentration happening within the US stocks.
Just across the world, there's concentration in every market.
But if you just kind of just look at international, the valuations tend to be much lower than
the US, people say that the US tech, nowhere else, that you can find the smart people to
create these big companies, like the SpaceX of the worlds.
And what's your rebuttal there?
You could have said the exact same thing 100 years ago, and instead of saying rockets and

You had one of the biggest bubbles we had ever seen at the time when the railroads and

But to them that wasn't boring.
That was the new technology the day.
They got into P ratios of 50 and 60.
And then you had the biggest decline we ever had in US stocks went down 80, 80% over 80.
The point there is not that, you know, you're going to have a crash, everyone's always crash

It's great.
It's a feature.

And so, you know, it changes over time, but that's the natural state of affairs and markets,
you know.

A lot of these companies fall off and now you talk about Apple and Amazon.
And all we talk about in 20 years, probably something else.

The US is the world's premier capital market and economy.
Two thirds of the world market cap, but only 25% of world GDP.
So there's a lot of countries out there, a lot of companies that are fantastic outside

And we say it's important.
Look, I love the US, but most of my money in the stock market here, but that doesn't

And what you put all your money in the US, that's something called a home country bias.
But it happens everywhere.
The Japanese do it, the Brits do it, the Aussies do it, on and on.
And it's usually, historically speaking, a horrible, terrible, no good, very bad idea.
Because on average, diversifying globally, you get lower risk, lower draw downs, and still

It could come from Brazil.
It could come from Africa, Europe, you don't know.

I mean, Samsung, SK, Hynex, a huge, I think Samsung's supposed to be the second most profitable

I mean, do you think that's going to lure people from there?

The long history of US versus XUS, so we call it foreign, but markets is one of waxing

So pat yourself on the back, drink some champagne, enjoy it and celebrate, but say, look,

And if you look at the rest of the world, the good times usually lead to less good times

And it said differently, when things have been terrible, and markets have gone nowhere for

I said, close your eyes.
There's no way you can possibly fathom how much the South Korean market is up in the

They're cheap.
They're hated.
And they just go from everything being terrible to being slightly less terrible.
You can get pretty explosive returns.
So you're seeing a reversion a little bit in the last year or two of things outside the
US.
The US has still done great.
Did 17% last year, up this year, but you've seen a resurgence in some of these markets
as well.
The beginning of this year, a lot of companies, the sentiment was that AI is going to disrupt

I think the big portion of the IT sector, some of the conductors still doing really well,

What are your thoughts on that?
Is that going to continue?

AI will change everything, right?

I like 80 or 90% of what I would say.
It's amazing.
The amount of fun ideas and content, I mean, I don't use Google anymore.
I just talked to my AI.

And yet, the stocks went nowhere because they just got too expensive, right?
So you have to make a distinction between the business and the stock.
Sometimes you can have the world's best business, but the stock is dear.
Sometimes you can have not a great business, but the stock is so cheap, it actually is a

You have these cycles that have lasted as long as capital markets and investing in stocks

So we have 200 years of these booms and busts outside the US before the US even was around.
And we find similar sort of sentiment ideas.
People get excited.
They push something up too much and then, you know, the inevitable fallout.
But that's the story of humans, right?
Like we were emotional.
We're not meant to trade shares of IBM and Apple and wheat futures and things like that.
We're meant to evolve on the savannah or something and run away from the lion.
So it seems scary and risky.
But if you zoom out, you take a diversified approach, it makes it a lot easier.
Well, you mentioned just the amount of supply over the last 40, 50 years, the amount of people
that own stocks has gone up five X.
I think you mentioned in 1980s, it was about 8% or 10% now with 50%.
And then hence, the supply of companies has actually gone down by half.
So is that, you know, you think more companies going public is going to maybe cause some pullback

First of all, I want to say I want 100% of Americans to own stock.
It is the greatest wealth building concept in history.

Well, you can.
And the beauty of it doesn't take that much work.
So you can buy a share in all these companies and participate in this amazing experiment,
which has been capitalism.
And so the long history of that is we can find, is it roughly stocks do like 10% a year.
And so I'm conflicted because in the US, I want everyone to be an investor.
And if you ask people around the world, there's been surveys, this is I think in the journal,
they say it is a good idea of the entrepreneur.
And it's like 90% of Americans are like, yeah, right?
And you ask people in Europe or in Asia, it's like 9%.
And if you look at their allocations in Europe or in Asia on and on,
a lot of places, it's cash in real estate.
And so that creates a wedge that just only gets bigger and bigger over time as markets compound.
So it's a fantastic idea for Americans to be interested in the stock market.
However, asterisk.
The problem is in our country, we don't teach money in investing in school.
It's getting more and more.
I didn't learn about it, you know, in a home app or taking calculus in high school, right?
And then we're up to about a third of high schools teach one class.
And even then, it's like a stockpicking class, so not the best education.
And I struggled a little bit during this last cycle and everyone stuck at home,
all these young investors started to learn about the market.
Great.
But they started to learn about the market through the casino.
Hey, let me teach you about hyperactive trading.
And now it's prediction markets.
You can bet on what the next pitch is going to be on the baseball game.
And so we said, no, that's the wrong lesson to take away.
You can invest in the broad stock market, own parts of these businesses.
And if you zoom out over 25 years, you're going to 10 extra money.
50 years, you're going to 100 extra money.
What a magical opportunity, but you can't mess it up, right?
And so the biggest struggle for most people, you guys know this, 2008, 2009,
talked to so many people, said, I sold everything, I couldn't take it anymore.
And once you've been through a few of these and you study history,
which is a big benefit, you start to learn that, hey,
the bear markets are a feature, right?
That's not a bug and being able to withstand that in many cases,
deploy capital during those periods is a fantastic opportunity.
We often tell young people, I went on TV not too long ago
and the market was down a bunch.
And I said, you know, the stock market is down.
This is fantastic for investors.
And they're like, what are you talking about?
I said, well, to be clear, young investors, right?
The best thing for a young investor, the market goes down a bunch,
because zero goes nowhere for 10 years.
Now, old investors, sorry, it's the opposite, right?
But these opportunities happen, it's normal.
I think Charlie Munger says, if you're now willing to take 50% declines,
then you should expect a mediocre returns.
Yeah.
And but now, obviously, you know, 20, 30 years ago,
you had a call broker, you say, I want to buy Apple stock.
She was going to call you, cost you 60 bucks.
Now, you can just do buying sale right on your phone.
And it kind of seems when the market does dip,
people just buy the dip, laser eyes.
So what do you think about that?
It's funny, you know, we wrote an article years ago
where we talked about knowledge evolving over time.
And I joked about the food pyramid of our youth.
And I said, the food period, when I was a kid,
was like, you should eat the most frosted flakes, pasta,
muffins, you know, at all the carbs,
and at the very, you definitely don't eat meat and fats, right?
It was like, it was loaded up as a much sugar as you want.
And then it was really funny,
because we wrote this paper years ago,
and we talked about the investing period,
a pyramid of our parents' generation,
how they thought about markets,
and then how much that's changed over the 10, 20, 30, 40,
50 years since then.
And then we talked about the food pyramid today.
I was like, it's been inverted.
And then they recently came out,
and they essentially inverted it, which was hilarious,
because we wrote about this.
And I said, the pyramid today looks totally different
than our parents' generation.
But that's good.
You know, you acknowledge compounds over time.
You start to learn, hey, things are important,
like taxes and fees, and what's most important?
Not to emotionally totally mess this up.
You know, freak out at the wrong times.
To have a plan.
You know, that's a big one.
90 plus percent of investors I talk to have no plan.
They just wing it.
And we know anything about markets and money and investing.
It's emotional, right?
If you're normal and stressor and most marriages is money.
And so coming up with investing markets and understanding,
like you see the geopolitical headlines.
They're always negative, right?
They're always crazy every day.
And it's even worse today with social media and amplifies.
Like back when you just had the Omaha newspaper,
like, what's the worst thing could happen?
Maybe somebody broken into a local convenience store.
But you amplify that to every city in the world.
Something horrible happens somewhere everywhere every day.
That's a thousand times worse than what could possibly happen in one city.
And so, you know, I think the long arc of history.
You guys got to hear it last night.
And I got to read from my new book.
And we talked about, you know, yes, people have been through hard times.
It's always the case.
But you look back in history and things have been worse.
And things have been way worse.
World wars, pandemics, depressions on and on.
And so I think one of the big parts in addition to having a plan
is trying to have at least a, you know, decent understanding of history
because that lets you put something in the perspective and say,
hey, you know, this tool will pass.
I think Morgan Halzaz is a nice quote about just like optimism.
Sounds like it's like a sales pitch.
And pessimism sounds like someone's trying to rescue you.
And so that's why people look at CNBC or all these other financial media
or their friends and say, hey, maybe it's time to get out.
And you can do something.
And I think Jack Bogle arrested Saul.
He says, don't just stand there.
Don't just do something.
Don't just do something.
Don't just do something, stand there.
Yeah, yeah.
So a big fan of that.
Okay.
I do want to switch to taxes.
A topic that you talked about yesterday.
Investors love dividends.
They love the warm comfort of money just coming in.
And thinking like the money just the stock will continue to go up.
And I'm just getting paid a coupon for holding it.
And obviously, that's not the case.
If you look at the long arc of investing in the stock market,
right, you see this beautiful chart of it going up over time.
And you say, hey, look, there's been this dividend yield,
which historically, four percent, let's call it,
but much higher than the one percent we hit today,
which is the lowest it's ever been.
And people say, oh, you know, great, I get to take that dividend
and I get to go spend it on Pina Claude's and Cabo
or go buy a new vacation house or spend it.
We're like, well, actually, to get the 10 percent return
of the stock market, you have to reinvest those dividends.
Like you don't get both.
That's not how this works.
And so I think it's, again, going back to the challenge of our world.
There's so much jargon, there's an education gap.
But realizing that dividends is simply a company returning cash flows
to the investor and its return to capital.
And so if you are a hundred dollar stock and you got a five percent dividend,
that stock's going to go down to 95.
And I think people don't appreciate that.
There's no free lunch and investing.
Now, dividends, often the reason people like them,
is they are assigned historically that a company is profitable,
that a company has excess earnings, that they have cash flows.
But a lot's changed in the last 30 years too,
companies often implement buybacks,
which actually exceed dividends in every year since the late 90s.
Now, there's so many myths and misunderstandings when it comes to buybacks.
We wrote an entire book on this.
It's free online.
You can get it shareholder yield.
And so shifting, this is a good example of having to take a holistic view
of something that's changed.
So incorporating both cash dividends plus net stock buybacks,
and how a company uses its cash and returns it to shareholders,
you get a more holistic view.
So when you take into account buybacks,
the number gets much closer to the historical dividend yield.
So it doesn't look as scary as saying there's no dividend yield on the stock market.
But in general, yeah, I think the most important thing is to stay invested.
And if you're compounding, you need to reinvest it.
Hey, if you're retired, you want to go spend it, God bless, go ahead.
But as Buffett used to say, you know, he's a perfect example.
Berkshire never paid a dividend.
He said the one time he paid a dividend, he was in the bathroom,
like 60 years ago.
But he understands that.
And you know, he was in talking about the 80s about Berkshire has been buying back a ton of stock
over the past five years.
So he gets this valuation arbitrage if the stock is cheap.
Now you do want to avoid a company wasting money
if it stocks super expensive buying back shares.
But that's actually pretty rare.
You actually don't see that that much.
So thoughtful capital allocation has always been important,
because CEOs can all do all sorts of crazy thing, name stadiums,
pay themselves a ton of money on and on due projects that go nowhere.
But it's the goal, the true role of the CEO to optimize that mix.
The one that everyone focuses on reinvesting in the business,
new products, new ideas, new sales.
But the other ways are just as important to the return of the shareholder.
I was actually reading an article this week.
I think it was Christine was saying she was always in total return.
She's like, hey, I'm going to focus on total return.
And then she's like, I recently changed my mind.
And I'm more for dividends.
And she said the reason why is people just hate to sell stocks.
When they have stocks, they don't want to sell them.
And so it's the way of kind of automating this company is going to sell it for you
and they're going to give you a check.
Yeah. And if you're spending it great.
And so the problem is, like Buffett used to say, he's like,
look, the best you can do is take out stocks certificate and fondle it.
He's like, you get nothing from this.
You can make your homemade dividend by selling a bit every quarter or every year, right?
But again, framing so much in our world, the narrative is so important.
The storytelling, how to frame investments.
And if you can frame something in a way that investors will behave better,
even if it's not optimal, that's okay.
And we talk a lot about this, how to set up structures and incentives and ideas.
And if that works, and it's not the best possible outcome, fine.
But if it's better than someone selling it all,
when things go down or having a worse outcome, you know,
so much is lost in our world of investors trying to go from,
hey, can I get 10% returns instead of 8?
And they're going to take on a ton more risk and do crazy things.
And the question is not, is it going to be 8 or 10?
The question is, is it 8 or 0?
Because they got taken out of the game.
And the whole key to all this, I mean, he go back to the purpose
than what you guys do.
What's the money for in the first place?
You know, there's like, you know, I think somebody is saying like money,
money is like filling up your car with gas,
but you're not doing a tour of gas stations, right?
Like you're not just accumulating as much as you can,
to my mom used to always say, you can't take it with you, right?
But getting to the finish line,
and even then, so many investors, they win the game,
and they still continue to make really aggressive bets.
So thinking about the purpose of it too,
but getting to the finish line, we often say the best compliment.
You can give someone in our world of investing.
This is also true in entrepreneurship.
It's also true in life, is that you make it to the finish line, right?
You don't get taken out of the game.
Because if you've ever been to Vegas or to poker table,
you lose all your chips, you can't play, right?
And so, so many mistakes, again, we're in Omaha,
so this feels fair to be quote in these two,
these two fellows, but you know, leverage,
Charlie's to say liquor, ladies, and leverage.
But the more leverage being won,
like taking on more risks than you really need,
I think is a huge mistake.
Talk to us about shareholder yield, how that's probably
a little bit better way to think about,
instead of just focusing on dividends, focusing on the whole.
If you think about like, you know, first principles,
and think about what are you trying to get when you're investing?
And this is perfect in Omaha.
Talk about Buffett.
Say, what would you want?
You're buying a business.
You want high cash flows.
You want the company to be distributing and treating shareholders fairly
and distributing cash flows.
You want to be a quality business.
All of these things make sense.
That you don't necessarily get in a broad index.
Broad index, you kind of get the entire haystack,
which is fine.
So you're guaranteed to own the winners,
but you're also going to own the losers, right?
And the Achilles heel on average of that broad index
is that it can be highly concentrated
in times of a euphoric period when things go totally nutty
to the upside.
And so, shareholder yield is an approach that says,
okay, we want to target companies that have high aggregate
cash dividend and include net stock buybacks.
Now, the US has really been the leader on this,
you know, starting in the 1990s.
There's a big myth that buybacks used to be illegal.
That's totally untrue.
Companies have been doing this for forever.
In fact, a hundred years ago, some of the investments,
the investment committees would require the companies
either pay a dividend or buyback shares
because guess what?
They didn't want the CEO's wasting money.
So, I have a book from, I think it's the 1960s
or 70s talking about the entire book is about share repurchases.
But what has changed really starting in the 90s,
CEOs started to understand the power of buybacks.
So, they started buying back more stock.
So, in the US, companies buy back more stock
than they pay out in dividends.
So, if you don't account for that,
that means you're ignoring half of the way the companies
distribute their cashflow as their shareholders,
which to me is crazy.
Now, the rest of the world is starting to catch up.
So, you're seeing a huge change in countries like Japan, China, UK
that have started doing a lot more buybacks over the past decade.
And so, why does this matter?
It matters not just from the buyback component.
It also matters from the share issuance.
And you got to remember to Charlie's to say
you want the cannibals, the companies eating themselves,
reducing the share count.
Because if you have a company that's consistently issuing shares,
you're getting diluted, right?
You're owning less and less of the business.
Traditionally, my home state of California,
CEOs love to do this.
They love to pay themselves a ton of stock-based compensation.
So, every year, you're getting diluted.
And let me give you an example.
Let's say someone does a screen and they say,
oh, great, I found this.
Perfect.
High dividend stock.
4% yielder.
Amazing.
And you don't include what they're doing with buybacks and issuance.
Theoretically, that company could say,
hey, we're issuing 5% shares per year.
We're not buying back shares.
That company has a negative yield.
And how many people know that?
Very, that are buying the stock for that.
You have very few.
So, you've got to consider both one to yes.
We want the companies that are cheap,
buying stock at 80 cents on the dollar,
but also to avoid the ones that are consistently issuing shares.
And the average stock in the stock market is a share issuer.

I mean, we think, we think Japan's attractive.

How is the narrative shifts for the older people listening to this,
the gray hairs, the no hairs that lived through the 1980s.

but that's not true.

Every company, Sony, Mitsubishi, on and on just dominated
